name: Parallel Work Partitioning Routine
description: Analyzes a set of work items and their dependencies, partitions them into independent batches that can be safely parallelized across subagents, and produces a sorted execution plan. Flags items with cross-dependencies for sequential handling by the main agent.
metaGuidance: INDEPENDENCE IS THE PRIORITY: a batch is only parallel-safe if no item in it depends on any other item in any concurrent batch.
CONSERVATIVE CLASSIFICATION: when dependency is unclear, classify as sequential. Wrong parallelization is worse than missed parallelization.
MAIN AGENT OWNS HARD WORK: items needing judgment, design decisions, or cross-cutting context belong to the main agent, not subagents.

steps.title: Step 1: Inventory Work Items
steps.prompt:
List every work item in scope.

For each item, capture:
- Item identifier (file path, module name, test case, doc section, etc.)
- One-line description of what needs to be done
- Estimated complexity: trivial / moderate / complex
- Whether it requires judgment or design decisions (yes/no)

Don't classify or sort yet. Just get the full inventory.

Capture:
- `workItems` (the full list)
- `totalCount`

steps.title: Step 2: Map Dependencies
steps.prompt:
For each work item, identify its dependencies on other items in the inventory.

A dependency exists when:
- Item B imports, references, or extends something in Item A
- Item B's output format or API is consumed by Item A
- Item B cannot be correctly completed without Item A being done first
- Item B shares mutable state, configuration, or resources with Item A

A dependency does NOT exist just because:
- Items are in the same module or directory
- Items use the same library or framework
- Items are conceptually related but structurally independent

Produce a dependency map: for each item, list which other items it depends on (if any). Items with no dependencies on other in-scope items are roots.

Capture:
- `dependencyMap`
- `rootItems` (items with no in-scope dependencies)
- `crossCuttingItems` (items that many others depend on)

steps.title: Step 3: Classify Parallel vs. Sequential
steps.prompt:
Classify each work item into one of two tracks:

**Parallel track** — can be delegated to a subagent. ALL of these must be true:
- No unresolved dependencies on other in-scope items (or all dependencies are already completed/in an earlier batch)
- Trivial or moderate complexity
- Does NOT require judgment, design decisions, or cross-cutting context
- Can be verified independently (build, test, or review in isolation)
- Failure is cheap to fix (a bad result can be caught and redone)

**Sequential track** — must be handled by the main agent. ANY of these makes it sequential:
- Has dependencies on items that haven't been completed yet
- Complex or requires design decisions
- Touches cross-cutting concerns (shared state, configuration, APIs used by many items)
- Needs context from other items to be done correctly
- Failure is expensive (wrong output cascades to other items)

When in doubt, classify as sequential.

Capture:
- `parallelItems`
- `sequentialItems`
- `classificationRationale` (one line per item explaining the decision)

steps.title: Step 4: Form Parallel Batches
steps.prompt:
Group parallel-track items into batches that can run concurrently.

Batching rules:
- No two items in the same batch should depend on each other
- No two items in the same batch should write to the same file or resource
- Keep batches between 3-5 items for manageable subagent scope
- Items that share a common dependency should be in the same batch or in batches that run after that dependency is resolved
- Prefer grouping items by similarity (same file type, same kind of work) so each subagent gets coherent instructions

Also sort sequential-track items by dependency order (dependencies first).

Capture:
- `parallelBatches` (array of batches, each with item list and subagent instructions summary)
- `sequentialOrder` (sorted list of sequential items)
- `executionPlan` (the full plan: which parallel batches run first, then which sequential items, noting any sync points)

steps.title: Step 5: Produce Execution Plan
steps.prompt:
Write the final execution plan as a structured artifact in {deliverableName}.

Structure:

```markdown
# Parallel Work Partitioning Plan

## Summary
- Total items: [count]
- Parallel track: [count] items in [N] batches
- Sequential track: [count] items
- Cross-cutting items: [list]

## Execution Order

### Wave 1: Parallel Batches (delegate to subagents)

#### Batch 1: [theme/description]
- [item]: [one-line task]
- [item]: [one-line task]
**Subagent instructions**: [what the subagent needs to know]

#### Batch 2: ...

### Wave 2: Sequential Items (main agent)
1. [item]: [one-line task] — depends on: [items]
2. [item]: [one-line task] — depends on: [items]

## Dependency Graph
[Compact representation of which items depend on which]

## Risk Notes
- [Any items where classification was uncertain]
- [Any sync points where parallel work must complete before sequential work starts]
```

Capture:
- `planDelivered`
